Matthew reveals he lost re-election for county sheriff earlier that year. Though the primary vote took place nine months prior in March, the transfer of office does not occur until January 5th, less than two weeks after this incident. Matthew was elected county sheriff the last two terms for a total of eight years. In the March 2024 Republican primaries, Matthew lost the election by just nine votes, giving Chad Burrows the victory and effectively removing Matthew from his position come January next year. Though Matthew only narrowly lost, his alleged questionable practices may reflect the public's growing frustration behind his leadership. One case, notably occurring after the primary election, involves Matthew arresting St. Paris Police Chief Eric Smith at the Champagne County Fair in August of 2024, making Matthew the second known officer arrest in the county in a matter of roughly four months. Off-duty, out of uniform and out of jurisdiction, Eric was arrested by Matthew at a privately run county fair for trespassing and obstruction after refusing multiple times to store his handgun in compliance with the fair's no-gun policy. The charges were later dropped, likely due to Ohio's House Bill 228, which permits officers to open carry if their badge is displayed, something Eric had, despite being casually dressed. St. Paris, which hired Eric as police chief in 2021, placed him on paid leave after the fair incident and eventually disbanded the entire department. Mayor Susan Prince cited finances and officer retention as their reasons and cited five officers had quit within seven months leaving only Eric, who spent at least two months on paid leave as the village's sole officer. When asked by constituents who they could rely on for law enforcement, the mayor informed they would utilize the county sheriff's office led by Matthew himself, as the village had been compelled to do since the St. Paris Police Department was understaffed. Eric was formally laid off on December 17th after the disbandment, which happens to be the same night Urbana officers arrested Matthew for driving under the influence.

Matthew faces one charge of operating a vehicle under the influence, a first-degree misdemeanor, and has pled not guilty. He was released on a recognizance bond with a one-year driving suspension under implied consent laws, meaning he cannot drive until at least December 20th, 2025. The original prosecutor for the case recused himself, likely due to conflict of interest, and a special prosecutor was assigned instead. The case is still pending trial, during which time Sergeant Sean retired from the force after working in Urbana for 25 years. Matthew faces up to six months in jail, fines up to $1,075, and a license suspension of up to three years, though he may be allowed to drive with an ignition interlock device. If caught driving on a suspended license, he must serve three days in jail, at least one month of house arrest, and could face up to six months of confinement, up to $1,000 in fines, a 30-day vehicle impound, and an additional one-year license suspension.
